event_blocks.summary: A whimsical, accordion-like melody accompanies the opening title card 'СОЮЗМУЛЬТФИЛЬМ' and the title 'ТРОЕ ИЗ ПРОСТОКВАШИНО' with its upbeat theme song, followed by credits. In a sunlit stairwell, a gray striped cat observes the city before jumping down and approaching the window. A red-haired boy, Uncle Fyodor, descends the stairs and offers food to the cat, Matroskin, who advises him on eating a sandwich correctly. Matroskin reveals his attic home is under renovation and invites Uncle Fyodor to live with him, expressing concern about his mother's approval, which Uncle Fyodor dismisses. The scene shifts to a cozy living room where Uncle Fyodor reads on a green couch, and Matroskin, wrapped in a black and white striped blanket, eats from a plate with a milk bottle. A red-haired man and woman enter; the woman senses the cat's presence, and a playful debate ensues about the cat's usefulness, with the man defending it by comparing it to a framed still-life painting that covers a hole in the wallpaper. The woman issues an ultimatum: the cat or her. The man playfully joins them on the couch, maintaining a warm familial atmosphere.

gray striped cat.description: A gray cat with white stripes, white muzzle, and chest, initially sitting on the windowsill, appearing hesitant and then accepting food from the boy, later seen eating on a couch wrapped in a black and white striped blanket; it is the subject of a playful debate between the man and woman regarding its usefulness, with the woman implying it was brought by 'Uncle Fyodor' and the man defending its presence.

black and white striped blanket.description: A cozy blanket with black and white stripes, draped over the cat as it eats on the couch.

red-haired man.description: A man with red hair and a green sweater with a white pattern, entering the room with the woman, placing bread and milk on a table, and later playfully jumping onto the couch; he is a male speaker who defends the cat, suggests it can catch mice, and tries to de-escalate the woman's ultimatum, possibly being the 'Uncle Fyodor' mentioned by the woman.

red-haired woman.description: A woman with red hair, glasses, and a pink blouse with a blue bow, entering the room with the man, holding a white cloth, gesturing toward the cat, and smiling at the family interaction; she is a female speaker who is critical of the cat's presence and usefulness, states the framed painting covers a hole in the wallpaper, and gives an ultimatum about the cat.

framed still-life painting.description: A golden-framed painting above the couch depicting a still life with oranges, a dark book, and a blue mug on a white surface; it becomes a focal point during the family's interaction and is mentioned by the woman as serving the practical purpose of covering a hole in the wallpaper.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a black screen displaying the white Cyrillic text 'СОЮЗМУЛЬТФИЛЬМ' as a whimsical, accordion-like melody begins to play. The scene transitions to a sunlit stairwell with a patterned floor and an arched window overlooking city buildings. A gray striped cat with a white muzzle and chest sits on the windowsill, looking out, then turns and jumps down to the floor, moving toward the window, all while the playful melody continues. The title card 'ТРОЕ ИЗ ПРОСТОКВАШИНО' appears in bold yellow letters on a blue background, accompanied by a more prominent and upbeat theme song. Credits follow, listing the scriptwriter, director, composer, and other crew members in yellow Cyrillic text on the same blue background, with the cheerful theme music continuing. After the credits, the scene returns to the stairwell, where a red-haired boy in a yellow shirt and blue pants descends the stairs, holding a piece of food. He stops and looks up at the cat, who is now perched on the windowsill again, smiling. The boy gestures toward the cat, seemingly offering the food, as the cat looks down at him with a friendly expression and a male voice states, "Неправильно ты, дядя Фёдор, бутерброд ешь. Ты его..." (You're eating your sandwich wrong, Uncle Fyodor. You...).

The scene opens with a red-haired boy, identified as 'Дядя Фёдор' (Uncle Fyodor) by his own voice, wearing a yellow shirt and blue pants, standing by a window and offering food to a gray striped cat, 'Кот Матроскин' (Cat Matroskin), who sits on the windowsill. The cat, speaking in a distinct male voice, advises the boy on how to properly hold the sausage for better taste, explaining he knows everyone in the house because he lives in the attic and can see everything, also revealing his attic is currently under renovation, leaving him nowhere to live. Initially hesitant and appearing stubborn, the cat's expression softens after the boy's smiling gesture, and he accepts the food with a pleased look. The boy then asks if he wants more food, confirming there's more at home, and invites the cat to live with him. The cat expresses concern that the boy's mother might object, but the boy reassures him, suggesting his father would stand up for them. The scene transitions to a cozy living room with red curtains, where 'Дядя Фёдор' is now on a green couch, reading a book with a cat on the cover, while 'Кот Матроскин' sits beside him, wrapped in a black and white striped blanket, eating from a plate with a milk bottle nearby. Gentle, whimsical instrumental music begins to play, underscoring the shift from cautious interaction to warm companionship and a growing bond between the boy and the cat.

A whimsical, slightly melancholic instrumental tune plays as the scene opens in a cozy living room, where a red-haired boy reads a book with a cat cover on a green couch. Beside him, a gray striped cat, wrapped in a black and white striped blanket, eats from a plate with a milk bottle, having initially appeared hesitant before accepting food from the boy's gesture. The door then opens, and a red-haired man in a green sweater and a red-haired woman with glasses and a pink blouse enter, smiling. The woman's voice is heard remarking, "Something smells like cat spirit here. No doubt, Uncle Fyodor brought the cat." The man's voice responds, "So what? One cat won't bother us," to which the woman retorts, "Well, it won't bother you, but it will bother me." The man moves to a white tablecloth-covered table, placing a loaf of bread and a milk bottle, while the woman walks to a wooden cabinet, holding a white cloth and gesturing as if giving instructions. The woman then questions the cat's usefulness, asking, "What's the use of this cat?" The man counters, "Why necessarily use? What's the use of this painting on the wall, for example?" as the camera focuses on a framed still-life painting above the couch. The woman explains, "But from this painting on the wall, there's a very big use. It covers a hole in the wallpaper." The man insists, "And the cat will be useful. It can catch mice," but the woman states, "We don't have mice." The man playfully suggests, "And we'll get some." The family gathers around the couch, with the man pointing at the painting and the woman smiling and gesturing toward the cat. The woman then gives an ultimatum, "If this cat is so important to you, please choose: either him or me," to which the man responds with a placating "No, no, no, oh, oh." The man then playfully jumps onto the couch, landing beside the boy and cat, as the woman watches with amusement, maintaining a warm and familial atmosphere despite the playful debate.

event_blocks.summary: A red-haired boy, Fyodor, sits on a green couch with a gray striped cat wrapped in a black and white striped blanket, holding a book with a cat cover and speaking about choosing someone. His parents, a red-haired man and woman, enter; the man playfully jumps on the couch, startling the cat, while the woman stands by a wooden cabinet. Fyodor, now standing, looks embarrassed and thoughtful. He is later seen kneeling at a white tablecloth-covered table, writing a letter with a red pen to his parents, stating his love for animals and his decision to leave for the village because they don't allow him pets. The gray striped cat rummages through a brown sack, pulling out kitchen items and knocking over a kettle, while Fyodor continues writing, signing off as 'Your son, Uncle Fyodor.' The scene shifts to an outdoor shot of a rural village, then to a dirt path leading to Prostokvashino, where Fyodor, carrying a staff, and the cat, Matroskin, carrying a sack, are met by a brown dog named Sharik. Sharik pleads to join them, and after some initial reluctance from Matroskin, Fyodor agrees to take him, stating a good dog never hurt anyone. Sharik excitedly offers to show them an empty house with a stove, and they all agree to live together. Introductions are made: Fyodor, Matroskin, and Sharik. The dog wags its tail, and the trio walks down the path toward the village, with green hills, a river, and colorful houses in the background.

raw_segments: The video opens with cheerful, whimsical instrumental music as a close-up shows a red-covered book titled 'КУДА' (Where To) featuring a black cat on its cover. A red-haired boy, identified as Fyodor, holds the book while sitting on a green couch beside a gray striped cat, who is wrapped in a black and white striped blanket and eating from a plate with a milk bottle nearby. Fyodor's young voice is heard saying, "Well, I choose you. I've known you for a long time," possibly referring to the cat or the book. The scene transitions to a wider shot of the living room as a red-haired man and red-haired woman enter, smiling and gesturing toward Fyodor and the cat. The man playfully jumps onto the green couch, causing the cat to flinch, while the woman stands by a wooden cabinet, holding a white cloth. Fyodor, now standing, holds the book and looks up at his parents with a slightly embarrassed expression, then looks down, appearing thoughtful or sad. The scene shifts to a different room where Fyodor is kneeling on a stool at a white tablecloth-covered table, intently writing on a large sheet of paper with a red pen. His voice narrates a letter to his "dear parents, dad and mom," stating, "I love you very much, but I also love animals, especially all sorts of cats!" A deeper voice (likely the red-haired man) interjects, confirming the boy's sentiment about cats. Fyodor continues, "But you don't let me have them. Therefore, I'm leaving for the village and I will live there." The gray striped cat sits beside him, mischievously rummaging through a brown sack and pulling out various kitchen items like a blue kettle and a yellow pot, occasionally glancing at the focused boy. The cat then playfully knocks over the kettle, causing a small spill, but Fyodor continues writing without interruption, signing off, "Your son, Uncle Fyodor." The deeper voice adds, "And the cat." The scene concludes with a wide outdoor shot of a rural village by a river, with colorful houses and a large white building atop a green hill, suggesting the destination of Fyodor's journey.

Against a cheerful, folksy musical backdrop, a rural landscape unfolds with a dirt path leading to a village, marked by a sign reading 'ПРОСТОКВАШИНО 100 м'. The red-haired boy, D дя Фёдор, dressed in a yellow shirt and blue pants, stands on the path holding a wooden staff, accompanied by the gray striped cat, Matroskin, who carries a brown sack on a stick over his shoulder. A brown dog, Sharik, with floppy ears and a white muzzle, suddenly runs up the path, stopping to sit and look up at them with an expressive, pleading face. Sharik, in an eager male voice, greets them and asks, "Здравствуйте! Возьмите меня к себе жить! Я вам буду всё охранять." Matroskin, with a slightly sarcastic male voice, retorts, "Ещё чего. Мы же сами нигде не живём. Так нам через год прибегай, когда мы хозяйством мы бы заведёмся." D дя Фёдор gently pats Sharik’s head, telling Matroskin to be quiet, stating, "Хороший собака ещё никому не мешала," and suggests, "Давай лучше узнаем, нет ли в деревне свободного домика." Sharik eagerly confirms, "Да есть, есть! Я вам один дом покажу. Там никто не живёт. Хозяева за реку переехали." Matroskin inquires, "А печка там есть?" to which Sharik enthusiastically replies, "У! В полкухни." Sharik then asks again if they will take him, and D дя Фёдор agrees, "Возьмём." Matroskin adds, "Возьмём! Втроём веселее." D дя Фёдор asks Sharik's name, who introduces himself as "Шарик. Я из простых собак, не из породистых." D дя Фёдор then introduces himself, "А меня Дядя Фёдор зовут," and Matroskin adds, "А кот Матроскин," clarifying, "Фамилия такой." Sharik responds, "Очень приятно." The dog then stands up, wagging its tail, and the boy, cat, and dog continue their journey, walking away from the camera along the path toward the village, set against green hills, a river, and colorful houses, creating a pastoral atmosphere.

event_blocks.summary: A whimsical accordion tune accompanies the exterior of a rustic house, where inside, a gray striped cat and brown dog playfully interact near a fireplace as a red-haired boy enters with a duster. The cat hides behind a green curtain before reappearing. A man with a mustache and brown hat peeks through the window, greeting them with "Будьте здоровы!" The boy responds "Спасибо!" and when asked about his origins, states, "I'm nobody's. I'm on my own, boy. My own. I came from the city." The man expresses disbelief, but the cat interjects, "Why doesn't it happen? I, for example, am a cat on my own. My own," followed by the dog's "And I'm my own!" The man, identified as Pechkin the postman, clarifies he's not from the police but needs to know everything for mail delivery, asking what they will subscribe to. The boy says he'll subscribe to "Murzilka." Later, at a wooden table with a golden samovar, blue teacups, and a plate of bread, a discussion ensues about needing milk and the logic of buying a cow. The cat playfully interacts with the dog, who is eating from a bowl, while the boy sips tea. The cat hides behind the green curtain again, reappearing to engage the surprised dog. The boy watches with amusement. The scene shifts to a dark, moonlit cave where the boy digs with a shovel, illuminated by a lantern, while the dog and cat watch from a rocky ledge. The boy explains they will buy a cow and everything at the market with treasure, humorously noting more bones are at the store. The cat becomes animated and leaps joyfully as the boy exclaims "Hooray!" In the cave, the boy, cat, and dog pull a wooden chest on wheels, shouting "Sklad!" A black bird flies in, startling them. A man with a mustache and brown hat emerges, demanding they "Отдай!" claiming the bird stole his "Olympic ruble." The boy protests, stating they will cure the bird and teach it to talk, explaining they were mushroom picking. The man, surprised, smiles, takes the bird, and places it on his shoulder. The boy, cat, and dog cheer. The scene transitions to a brightly lit kitchen where a red-haired woman scolds a red-haired man for spoiling someone, while he defends the person, stating, "He just loves animals" and "That's why he left with the cat."

raw_segments: The segment opens with a cheerful, whimsical accordion-like tune accompanying an exterior shot of a rustic wooden house, marked by a sign reading 'House is free, live whoever wants to'. Inside, the gray striped cat and brown dog playfully interact near a fireplace, holding a broom and stick respectively, as the red-haired boy enters with a duster and begins cleaning the simply furnished room. The mischievous cat hides behind a green curtain before reappearing with a grin. A man with a mustache and brown hat then peeks through the window, smiling and waving. The boy, dog, and cat look towards the man, who greets them with a distinct voice, saying "Будьте здоровы!" (Be healthy!), to which the boy responds "Спасибо!" (Thank you!). The man then questions the boy, asking "And you, boy, whose are you? Where did you come from to our village?", and the boy replies, "I'm nobody's. I'm on my own, boy. My own. I came from the city." The man expresses disbelief, stating, "That doesn't happen. For children to be on their own. Children are always someone's." As the cat strikes a confident, muscle-flexing pose, it interjects with a distinct voice, "Why doesn't it happen? I, for example, am a cat on my own. My own." The dog then chimes in with its own voice, "And I'm my own!", prompting the man to ask, "And you, actually, why are you interested? You're not from the police?" The cheerful music continues throughout this lively exchange in the cozy, lived-in room.

The scene opens with a man, identified by his voice as Pechkin, the local postman, peeking through a wooden house window. He wears a brown hat and yellow coat, smiling and waving cheerfully as he states, "No, I'm not from the police. I'm from the post office. I'm the local postman. Pechkin. That's why I need to know everything to deliver the mail," before asking what they will subscribe to. He then pulls his hat off, waves again, and disappears. Inside, a red-haired boy, holding a long-handled duster, stands beside a brown dog, both looking toward the window. The boy's voice is heard saying he will subscribe to "Murzilka," while other male voices discuss subscribing to hunting magazines or saving money. A gray striped cat, initially hiding behind a green curtain, emerges and strikes a confident pose with a mischievous grin. The scene transitions to a cozy dining area, featuring a patchwork curtain, a small TV, and a wooden fireplace, where the boy, dog, and cat are seated at a wooden table with a golden samovar, blue teacups, and a plate of bread. Upbeat, whimsical background music begins as a male voice ponders, "Why are we always without milk, without milk? One could die like that. We should buy a cow." Another male voice agrees, but the boy's voice asks, "But where to get the money?" The conversation continues among male voices, debating borrowing from neighbors, how to pay it back (with milk), and then the logic of buying a cow if the milk is given away, concluding that they should sell something unnecessary. Amidst this discussion, the cat playfully interacts with the dog, who is eating from a bowl, while the boy sips tea and smiles. The cat then hides behind the green curtain again, reappears, and continues to engage with the surprised dog. The boy watches with amusement, holding his teacup and smiling contentedly as the whimsical music plays out.

The scene opens in a cozy, rustic room where a brown dog and a gray striped cat share a meal at a wooden table, featuring a golden samovar, bread, and blue teacups against a patchwork curtain and night window. A male voice, identified as the brown dog, laments needing to buy something unnecessary to sell something unnecessary, as they have no money. Another male voice, belonging to the gray striped cat, playfully suggests, "And let's sell Sharik from you," to which the brown dog indignantly replies, "How so? Me?" The red-haired boy, wearing a yellow shirt and blue pants, then enters, pointing excitedly. A calm, authoritative male voice, belonging to the boy, declares, "We won't sell anyone. We'll go look for treasure," prompting an excited "Hooray!" from the boy. The gray striped cat, however, curiously asks, "And what is a warehouse?" The scene transitions to a dark, moonlit cave where the boy digs with a shovel, illuminated by a hanging lantern, accompanied by the distinct sound of shoveling. The brown dog and gray striped cat watch from a rocky ledge above, the cat initially concerned. The boy's voice continues, explaining the benefits of finding treasure: "Now we'll buy a cow. And we don't have to work in the garden. We can buy everything at the market. And in the store. It's better to buy meat at the store." The cat's voice questions, "Why?", and the boy's voice humorously responds, "There are more bones there." As the digging continues, the cat becomes increasingly animated, eventually leaping joyfully into the air with a wide grin, while the brown dog looks on with surprise and amusement, culminating in a final excited "Hooray!" from the boy, marking a moment of discovery and excitement.

The video opens in a dark, rocky cave, illuminated by a faint blue light, where the red-haired boy, the gray striped cat, and the brown dog are seen pulling a wooden chest on wheels, each holding a shovel. A triumphant male voice shouts "Sklad!" followed by a whoop. Suddenly, a black bird, identified as a jackdaw by its distinct squawks and flapping sounds, flies in, startling them. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a yellow coat, emerges from the shadows, dramatically gesturing as if to stop them. He confronts the group, sternly shouting "Отдай!" (Give it back!) twice, claiming the jackdaw stole his "Olympic ruble" and needs to be taken to a clinic for "experiments." The red-haired boy, speaking with a protesting voice, argues against this, stating they will cure the bird and teach it to talk, and explains they were "mushroom picking" with the chest. The man, initially stern, shifts to a surprised expression as the boy hands him the bird, then smiles warmly, takes the bird, and gently places it on his shoulder. The boy, cat (who lets out a distinct meow), and dog cheer joyfully, their shovels dropping. The scene transitions to a brightly lit kitchen with red cabinets and yellow tiled walls, where the red-haired woman, speaking with a scolding female voice, is drying dishes at a table, telling the red-haired man, who enters holding a white cloth and begins washing dishes at the sink, that "It's your fault" and he "spoiled" someone. The red-haired man, in turn, defends the person, stating in a male voice that "He just loves animals" and "That's why he left with the cat." The kitchen, adorned with hanging wooden spoons and utensils, presents a warm, domestic atmosphere, sharply contrasting with the earlier cave setting.

event_blocks.summary: In a brightly lit kitchen, a red-haired woman drying a plate converses with a red-haired man washing dishes, who suggests they need dogs, cats, and friends for hide-and-seek games to prevent children from disappearing. The woman counters that parents will disappear due to exhaustion and urges him to focus on finding the missing boy, Uncle Fyodor, who is one meter twenty tall. The man proposes printing a newspaper notice with his description. The scene shifts to a dimly lit wooden room where a gray striped cat watches a black bird outside a window. A clock ticks, followed by a bell. A brown dog enters, and an off-screen male voice questions why they are feeding it for nothing, urging it to be useful and sing songs. The cat gestures animatedly, and the dog joins it, both appearing to communicate about the bird. An excited male voice exclaims, 'Hooray! It worked!' The scene transitions to an exterior of a rustic wooden house, where the black bird, now inside, repeatedly asks 'Кто там?' (Who's there?) in a child-like voice as an upbeat, folksy tune plays. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and yellow coat, approaches carrying a white box with black squares. He stops, looks at the window, and reacts to something off-screen. The bird watches him nervously, continuing its query. The man, identifying himself as 'Postman Pechkin,' announces he has a note about the boy, eventually responding to the bird's questions with 'No one! It's me, Postman Pechkin!' The bird's persistent questioning creates a tense atmosphere around the man's significant arrival.

raw_segments: The video opens in a brightly lit kitchen with red cabinets and yellow tiled walls, where an upbeat, whimsical instrumental tune plays. A red-haired woman in a pink blouse with a blue bow and glasses is drying a blue-rimmed plate with a yellow cloth, gesturing toward a red-haired man in a green sweater with a white pattern who is washing dishes at the sink. The man, speaking in a male voice, suggests, "We need to have dogs and cats in the house, and a whole bag of friends, and all sorts of hide-and-seek games. Then children won't disappear." The woman, in a female voice, retorts, "Then the parents will start disappearing. Because I'm already tired at work. I barely have enough strength to watch TV. And don't tell me your nonsense! You'd better tell me how we can find the boy?" The man turns to face her, holding a white cloth, and places a clean plate on a stack on the white tablecloth-covered table, continuing, "We need to print a notice in the newspaper. That a boy is missing. His name is Uncle Fyodor, height one meter twenty. If anyone sees him, let them inform us." The scene then cuts to a dimly lit wooden room with a patchwork curtain and a window showing a green landscape. A gray striped cat with a white muzzle sits thoughtfully on the floor, then leaps onto a wooden bench by the window, watching a black bird with a yellow beak perched outside. A clock ticking sound is heard, followed by a bell. A brown dog with floppy ears and a white muzzle enters through a wooden door, looking around curiously. An off-screen male voice asks, "Oh, why are we feeding him for nothing? Let him be useful. Well, tell me, who's there? Who's there? Well, tell me, who's there? Who's there? Who's there?" as the dog stands on a striped rug, gesturing with its paws as if speaking, while the cat remains focused on the bird outside the window. The off-screen voice continues, "Ugh, don't you have anything to do? You'd better sing him songs..."

Inside a dimly lit wooden room, a gray striped cat sits on a bench beneath a window framed by pink floral curtains, intently watching a small black bird with a yellow beak perched on the windowsill, as two male voices discuss the utility of asking 'Who's there?' to deter strangers. The cat's expression shifts from curiosity to excitement, gesturing animatedly, while a brown dog with floppy ears and a white muzzle enters and joins the cat, both appearing to engage in a silent conversation, gesturing toward the bird. The bird looks around nervously, seemingly reacting to the animals' excitement and the ongoing discussion. Suddenly, an excited male voice exclaims, 'Hooray! It worked!' as the scene cuts to an exterior view of a rustic wooden house with blue shutters and a red roof. The same black bird is now visible inside the window, repeatedly asking 'Кто там?' (Who's there?) in a child-like voice. An upbeat, folksy tune with prominent accordion and clarinet begins to play. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a yellow coat, approaches the house carrying a white box with black squares. He stops at the door, looks up at the window, and turns to the side, seemingly reacting to something off-screen. The bird inside watches him with wide, nervous eyes, ducks down, reappears briefly, and disappears again, continuing its 'Кто там?' query. The man, identifying himself as 'Postman Pechkin' in a distinct male voice, repeatedly announces that he has 'brought a note about your boy,' eventually responding with a slightly exasperated 'No one! It's me, Postman Pechkin!' as the bird continues to question his presence. The atmosphere shifts from playful curiosity inside to a more mysterious and slightly tense outdoor moment, suggesting the man’s arrival is significant to the bird’s fate.

event_blocks.summary: Postman Pechkin, a man with a mustache and brown hat, wearing a yellow coat, approached a rustic wooden house with blue shutters and a red roof, carrying a white box with black squares. A high-pitched, repeated "Кто там?" was heard, possibly from a black bird with a yellow beak peeking from a window with pink floral curtains. Postman Pechkin knocked, announcing, "Это я, почтальон Печкин, принёс заметку про вашего мальчика!", before suddenly collapsing to the ground with a thud, accompanied by background music. A red-haired boy, Uncle Fyodor, in a yellow shirt and blue pants, and a brown dog rushed to him. Uncle Fyodor picked up the box, revealing a newspaper with a Cyrillic missing child ad: "Пропал мальчик. Глаза голубые, рост метр двадцать, родители его ищут. Нашедшего ждёт премия - велосипед." The boy looked shocked. A gray striped cat with a white muzzle and chest, holding a stick, appeared and gestured excitedly. The brown dog then spoke, comforting Uncle Fyodor, "Не горюй, дядь Фёдор.", to which the cat added, "Да мало ли таких мальчиков. Может, и немало." Postman Pechkin then reappeared, standing by the door, pointing upward with a serious expression.

event_blocks.summary: A man with a mustache and brown hat, holding a yellow measuring tape, measures a door outside a wooden room, stating in Russian, "But bicycles are not given to everyone. Now I will measure your boy." Through pink floral curtains, a white cow with brown spots walks past a clothesline in a rural yard. Inside, a red-haired boy sits at a wooden table, writing on a large sheet of paper with a red pen, a white envelope and a small lamp beside him. He looks up, startled, as the cow reappears with a black bird on its back, accompanied by a bicycle bell. The boy exclaims, "My dad and mom. I live well. Simply wonderful. I have everything. Oh!" and rushes to the window. Upbeat, accordion-driven folk music begins as he returns to his seat, where a gray striped cat then sits and writes on the paper, followed by a brown dog also writing. The scene shifts to a living room with red curtains, where the folk music continues. A red-haired woman in a pink blouse and glasses sits on a green couch with a red-haired man in a green sweater wrapped in a black and white striped blanket. The woman gestures to a framed still-life painting above the couch, depicting a dark book, oranges, and a blue mug. A thought bubble appears above her head, showing a small red-haired boy looking out of a pink house window, suggesting she is imagining her son. The boy's voiceover repeats, "My dad and mom. I live well. Simply wonderful. I have everything. I have my own home." The man holds a piece of paper, pointing to the painting. The woman, wearing glasses, reacts with surprise as a thought bubble appears, showing a small red-haired boy clutching a white cloth, which then changes to show the boy smiling joyfully in a red knitted sweater. Gentle, melancholic accordion music plays as the woman reads a letter aloud in Russian from 'Uncle Sharik,' detailing his health, mentioning 'paws ache' and 'tail falls off,' but happily reporting he has started shedding, with 'old fur falling off' and 'new, clean, silky' fur growing, so his 'shagginess has increased.' The man, reclining under the blanket, sips from a white cup, appearing content. The woman finishes reading the letter, which concludes with 'Goodbye. Your son, Uncle Sharik.' The man asks, 'What has increased for him?' and the woman replies, 'Shagginess.'

event_blocks.summary: In a warmly lit living room, a distressed red-haired woman in a pink blouse and blue skirt, wearing glasses, speaks in Russian, questioning their sanity and ability to sleep in the snow, while a red-haired man in a green sweater listens and offers comfort. Their conversation is interrupted by the arrival of Postman Pechkin, who announces himself and inquires about giving away bicycles for a boy. The scene shifts to a dimly lit wooden room with patchwork and pink floral curtains, where a storm rages outside. A red-haired boy lies sick in bed with a thermometer and yellow scarf, attended by a concerned brown dog and gray striped cat. The dog, after examining the thermometer, dramatically scolds the cat in Russian for giving the boy cold milk. A knocking prompts a dialogue about 'our own' people, and the scene concludes with the closed wooden door, suggesting isolation amidst the storm. Later, in the same wooden room, the dog and cat express concern for the unwell boy. A gruff voice states that only strangers wander in such weather. The boy calls for his parents, and a man with a mustache, yellow coat, and brown hat enters with a red bicycle, followed by a red-haired woman in a green coat. A voice-over suggests handing them over for experiments. The woman checks the boy, who then sits up and smiles, receiving a red jar from the cat. The woman expresses surprise at the cat's intelligence, to which the man boasts about his own abilities. A black bird with a yellow beak is seen at a window before flying away. The scene transitions to the boy's parents deciding to take him to the city for care, with the man offering to let him take the cat, Matroskin, and the dog, Sharik. Sharik suggests they will stay and await visits. The man expresses concern for his farm and cow. The cat and dog interact playfully on a striped rug. Outdoors, by a blue car, the woman takes the black bird from the man, who urges them to stop. Pechkin arrives, announcing he has brought the Murzilka magazine, and the woman expresses embarrassment for forgetting him.

raw_segments: The video opens in a warmly lit living room with red curtains and a green couch, where a red-haired woman in a pink blouse and blue skirt, wearing glasses, appears distressed, gesturing with her hands as she speaks in Russian, wondering if they've gone crazy or if their 'shagginess' has increased, allowing them to sleep in the snow. A red-haired man in a green sweater with a white pattern listens, then places a comforting hand on her shoulder, logically countering in Russian that people go crazy individually, unlike getting the flu together. Their interaction is interrupted by the cheerful entrance of a man with a mustache, wearing a yellow coat and brown hat, who waves and announces himself in Russian as 'Postman Pechkin from Prostokvashino,' asking if they are the ones giving away bicycles for a boy. Gentle, melancholic instrumental music begins as the scene cuts to a dimly lit wooden room with a patchwork curtain and pink floral curtains framing a window, where a storm rages outside. Inside, a red-haired boy lies in bed, sick, with a thermometer in his mouth and a yellow scarf around his neck. A brown dog and a gray striped cat sit beside him, looking concerned. The music continues as the dog takes the thermometer, examines it, and reacts with alarm, dramatically gesturing and scolding the cat in Russian, blaming him for the boy's illness by giving him cold milk and even boasting about it. The cat defensively questions why he is being blamed. A knocking sound is heard, followed by a voice from outside asking 'Who's there?', to which a voice from inside replies 'It's us!', and the outside voice questions 'Our own?'. The final shot shows the closed wooden door of the room, with a red-handled latch, suggesting the characters are now isolated inside as the storm continues.

The scene opens in a dimly lit, cozy wooden room where a brown dog with floppy ears and a gray striped cat with green eyes sit on a dark surface, looking concerned. A deep, gruff male voice states, "In such weather, their own people sit at home, watch TV. Only strangers wander around. We won't open the door." The cat gestures animatedly, then raises its paw as if to speak. The camera shifts to a red-haired boy, appearing unwell, lying in a bed with a white metal headboard, covered by a dark blanket, wearing a white shirt and a yellow scarf. Beside him is a nightstand with a green bottle and a box of medicine, with a patchwork curtain to the right and a white curtain with colorful patches to the left. A child's urgent voice calls out, "Open up! It's my dad and mom who arrived." A door creaks open, and whimsical, accordion-led instrumental music begins as a man with a mustache, wearing a yellow coat and a brown hat, enters with a red bicycle, followed by a red-haired woman in a green coat and glasses. A theatrical, slightly sinister voice-over comments, "See what they've done to your child. They need to be immediately handed over to the clinic, and for experiments." The woman approaches the boy, gently touching his forehead and hand with concern. The boy then sits up, smiling, as the cat and dog sit beside him. The woman asks, "Where is your raspberry?" and the man replies, "Here it is, please." The cat, now holding a red jar, offers it to the boy, who takes it with a smile. The woman, surprised, exclaims, "Oh, I didn't even know cats could be so smart. I thought they only knew how to shout in trees." The man retorts, "Big deal. I can even embroider, and on a typewriter too," as the woman pets the cat affectionately. The scene transitions to a window with pink floral curtains, where a black bird with a yellow beak is perched, looking inside. The bird flaps its wings and hops on the windowsill, then flies away as the curtains flutter in the breeze, with the playful music continuing in the background, revealing a wooden fence and green trees under a cloudy sky outside.

The scene opens inside a rustic wooden room with a patchwork curtain, accompanied by upbeat, folksy music. A red-haired boy lies unwell under a dark blanket on a bed. A red-haired woman in a green coat and glasses stands beside him, gently touching his forehead and hand, telling him in a caring tone, "You, son, however you want, but we'll take you to the city. You need care." A red-haired man with a long nose and brown coat leans over a wooden stool, smiling warmly, adding encouragingly, "And if you want to take the cat or Sharik, or someone else? Take them!" The woman agrees, "We won't object." The boy sits up, smiling as he receives a red jar from a gray striped cat, named Matroskin by the man who says, "Well, Matroskin, you'll come with me." The cat, with a happy expression, sits beside the bed. The brown dog, named Sharik by the woman, sits nearby, looking on, and then speaks in a calm, reasonable voice, "We'll stay. You'd better come to us yourself. For holidays. And visit on weekends." The man then exclaims, "I'm off, Kabardin, damn it," and expresses concern for his farm, "And my cow, the farm, supplies for winter." The scene transitions to the floor, where the gray striped cat and brown dog sit on a striped rug, gesturing and interacting playfully. The setting then shifts outdoors to a rural yard with a blue car parked near a wooden fence. The red-haired woman, boy, and dog stand by the car as the man drives, holding a small black bird with a yellow beak. The man urgently calls out, "Stop! Stop!" before offering, "Here, take it. You'll have more fun with him." The woman takes the bird, cradling it gently, while the cat and dog look on with delight. The bird flaps its wings and hops, and the woman smiles, holding it close as the boy and dog watch with joy. The woman then asks, "Who's there?" as a new voice, belonging to postman Pechkin, announces, "It's me, postman Pechkin, I brought the Murzilka magazine." The woman responds with an embarrassed, "Oh, how awkward," to which Pechkin slightly reproaches, "You completely forgot about Pechkin." The atmosphere throughout is warm and affectionate, emphasizing family and animal companionship in a cozy, rural setting, all underscored by the continuous upbeat, folksy music.

event_blocks.summary: An upbeat, folksy instrumental tune accompanies the man with the mustache and brown hat, clad in a yellow coat, as he stands by a red bicycle with white handlebars in a rural setting. He gestures, mounts the bicycle, and smiles broadly, remarking, "And rightly so. He's so harmful." He then questions his own harmfulness, attributing it to not having a bicycle, and resolves to become kinder and acquire a pet, stating, "So life is merrier. You come home, and it's happy to see you." The scene shifts to the red-haired woman with glasses and a green coat, accompanied by the brown dog and gray striped cat, waving. The man reappears on the bicycle, now with the dog and cat riding alongside him, all waving joyfully to the sound of a bicycle bell. A male voice invites, "Come visit us in Prostokvashino!" A blue car passes a signpost reading 'ПРОСТОКВАШИНО 100 м'. The man, dog, and cat continue waving on the bicycle along a dirt path, the bell ringing again. The video ends with a black screen displaying 'КОНЕЦ' in yellow Cyrillic, followed by the 'СОЮЗМУЛЬТФИЛЬМ' logo and the title card 'КАНИКУЛЫ В ПРОСТОКВАШИНО' on a blue background, with credits for the scriptwriter, director, composer, and crew appearing in white Cyrillic on the same blue background, all underscored by the continuing cheerful instrumental music.

The scene shifts to a sunny rural yard with a wooden fence, green trees, and a rustic wooden house; a white cow with brown spots walks along a dirt path past a clothesline with laundry. event_blocks.summary: In a cozy wooden room with a red-and-white checkered tablecloth, a gray striped cat, speaking with a male voice, sits on a wooden stool, pouring a drink from a brown jug into a white mug and making a remark about eating mice, followed by a humorous suggestion about carbonated water. A brown dog, initially sad, becomes joyful and excitedly suggests 'kvass!' The cat retorts with a jab about the dog's intelligence and work ethic. The dog declares an intention to go hunting, prompting the cat to question his remorse for shooting small animals. The dog admits to feeling an instinctual pull to the forest, to which the cat responds with exasperated sounds and ironic agreement that animals are meant to be hunted. The dog excitedly promises a quick return with something tasty and expresses a wish for Uncle Fyodor to see him, before the cat dismissively calls him an 'unfortunate spaniel.' A melancholic piano melody begins as the scene shifts to a room with red floral wallpaper where a red-haired man reclines on a green couch under a black and white striped blanket, reading a newspaper. Beside him, a red-haired boy with glasses sits reading a book with Cyrillic letters, sounding out 'Кар!' as he learns letters, prompted by his father. A poster of the gray striped cat and brown dog, labeled 'МАТРОСКИН ШАРИК,' hangs on the wall. The scene then moves to a kitchen where a red-haired woman with glasses, initially joining the boy's letter game, becomes exasperated and declares her desire to leave for the resort the next day, complaining about her 'hopeless' life. The man calmly suggests their house in Prostokvashino is sufficient. A doorbell rings, and the boy asks who it is, to which the woman sarcastically replies that no one came and her husband won't let them go to the resort. The boy aligns with his father, stating he also wants to go to Prostokvashino. The woman, by a wooden wardrobe, pulls out a green suitcase and begins packing, vehemently arguing against Prostokvashino and emphasizing her year of working with a frying pan. The scene cuts back to the living room, showing the man gesturing toward the boy, who looks up from his book, while the woman continues packing a blue dress, her focused actions contrasting with the man's relaxed demeanor and the boy's quiet reading.

raw_segments: The scene opens in a cozy wooden room with a red-and-white checkered tablecloth. A gray striped cat, speaking with a distinct male voice, sits on a wooden stool, pouring a drink from a brown jug into a white mug, remarking, "And some even eat mice," before sighing and playfully suggesting, "And if your cow were smarter, it wouldn't give milk, but carbonated water. Pepsi-Cola, for example." Across the table, a brown dog, initially looking sad, shifts to a surprised and joyful expression, excitedly interjecting, "Oh, or kvass!" The cat then teases, "And if you were smarter, you would have started working a long time ago." The dog, with an eager male voice, declares, "And I will. I'm going hunting now," prompting a sigh from the cat, who then asks, "And aren't you sorry to shoot little animals?" The dog replies, "Sorry," but explains, "Well, if an instinct awakens in me, I'm drawn to the forest with terrible force." The cat reacts with exasperated sounds and a chuckle, ironically stating, "And animals, by the way, are created for that, to be hunted." The dog, excited, promises, "I'll be back soon, I'll shoot something tasty," and exclaims, "Oh, if only Uncle Fyodor saw me!" The cat dismisses him with a playful insult, "Go, go, you unfortunate spaniel." A gentle, melancholic piano melody then begins to play as the scene cuts to a different room with red floral wallpaper. Here, a red-haired man reclines on a green couch under a black and white striped blanket, reading a newspaper, while beside him, a red-haired boy with glasses sits, reading a book with large Cyrillic letters. A small TV and a framed still-life painting are visible, and a poster above the TV shows a drawing of the gray striped cat and brown dog, labeled 'МАТРОСКИН ШАРИК' (Matroskin Sharik). The boy points to the letter 'Ж' in his book, then looks up at the poster, seemingly connecting the characters in the book to the poster, shifting the atmosphere from playful domesticity to a quiet, studious moment, suggesting a narrative link between the characters in the two scenes.

In a cozy living room with red floral wallpaper, an upbeat, whimsical tune plays as a red-haired man, identified as 'папа' (dad), reclines on a green couch, reading a newspaper. Beside him, a red-haired boy in a yellow sweater sits on a black-and-white striped blanket, engrossed in a large book with Cyrillic letters 'ЖЗ'. The boy repeatedly sounds out 'Кар!' as he learns letters, encouraged by the man who prompts him to 'crow this letter'. A poster of a gray striped cat and a brown dog labeled 'МАТРОСКИН ШАРИК' hangs on the wall. The scene shifts to a kitchen where a red-haired woman with glasses, wearing a pink turtleneck, initially joins the boy's letter game with a playful 'Ну, а это-то какая?', but quickly becomes exasperated, declaring 'Хватит! Хватит, надоело!' She then dramatically complains about her 'hopeless' life, feeling like a 'serf peasant' with 'four evening silk dresses' but 'nowhere to wear them'. With a determined tone, she announces, 'We're leaving for the resort tomorrow!' The man calmly questions her, suggesting their 'wonderful house' in 'Prostokvashino' is sufficient. A doorbell rings, prompting the boy to excitedly ask 'Who's there?', to which the woman sarcastically replies, 'Nobody came. Our dad has gone crazy. He won't let us go to the resort.' The boy then aligns with his father, stating, 'I don't want to go to the resort either. I want to go to Prostokvashino!' The woman, now by a wooden wardrobe, pulls out a green suitcase and begins packing, vehemently arguing against Prostokvashino, asking what she would do there, and declaring, 'I've been working with a frying pan for you all year. And now I want to go to the resort!' The scene cuts back to the living room as the man gestures toward the boy, who looks up from his book, while the woman continues packing a blue dress, her focused actions contrasting the man's relaxed demeanor and the boy's quiet reading, all set against the backdrop of their domestic setting with a small TV and framed pictures.

event_blocks.summary: At a bustling train station, a red-haired woman in a blue suit and glasses, a bearded man in a green jacket, and their red-haired son, Fyodor, carrying luggage, walk along the platform as a station announcement for the Moscow-Sochi train departing from track four and the Prostokvashino electric train from track five is made, while a digital clock advances from 10:42 to 10:43. The family approaches the red Moscow-Sochi train, but Fyodor, holding a stick with a black bird, confidently boards the green 'МП' train to Prostokvashino, assuring his mother, "Мама! Мама! Ты за меня не беспокойся! Я с Матроскиным и Шариком не пропаду, мы горошком держим!" His mother, distressed, exclaims, "Послушай! А где же наш мальчик? Куда пропал дядя Фёдор? Я тебя воспитывала! Я из-за тебя ночей не спала! А ты... на электричке едешь!" The man calmly lights his pipe, exhaling smoke, and remarks, "Он правильно едет. Ему в деревне лучше. У него же нет вечерних платьев." The scene transitions to a serene rural landscape featuring a river, green trees, and rolling hills, where the black bird flies by, followed by a white horse pulling a wooden cart laden with boxes along the riverbank, all set to cheerful, folksy music.

raw_segments: The video opens at a bustling train station, where a red-haired woman in a blue suit and glasses, a bearded man in a green jacket, and their red-haired son, Fyodor, in a yellow sweater, walk along the platform with luggage. A station announcement, "Внимание, внимание! Поезд Москва-Сочи отходит с четвёртого пути. Электричка на Простоквашино с пятого," clarifies the signs for 'СОЧИ' and 'ПРОС' (Prostokvashino) as a digital clock ticks from 10:42 to 10:43. The family approaches the red 'МОСКВА-СОЧИ' train, but Fyodor, carrying a stick with a black bird, confidently boards the green 'МП' train to Prostokvashino, exclaiming, "Мама! Мама! Ты за меня не беспокойся! Я с Матроскиным и Шариком не пропаду, мы горошком держим!" The woman, frustrated, cries out, "Послушай! А где же наш мальчик? Куда пропал дядя Фёдор? Я тебя воспитывала! Я из-за тебя ночей не спала! А ты... на электричке едешь!" The man, however, calmly lights his pipe with a match, exhaling smoke thoughtfully as he states, "Он правильно едет. Ему в деревне лучше. У него же нет вечерних платьев." The scene then shifts to a serene rural landscape with a river, green trees, and rolling hills, where the black bird flies across the frame, followed by a white horse pulling a wooden cart loaded with boxes along the riverbank, all accompanied by cheerful, folksy music.

event_blocks.summary: Inside a rustic wooden house, a red-haired boy in a yellow sweater and blue pants enters carrying a green backpack, greeted by the excited exclamation of a red beaver, "Ah, finally, my beloved Uncle Fyodor has arrived! Now we'll save twice as much hay for our cow!" A gray striped cat with white stripes and a white muzzle jumps excitedly, and a small black bird with a yellow beak flies in through the open door, landing on the cat's back, startling it. The boy waves goodbye and steps outside. The scene shifts to the yard where the red beaver, holding a rifle and appearing mischievous, carries a brown dog with floppy ears and a white muzzle, dressed in a gray shirt, on its back. The beaver identifies the dog as "Sharik" and, in response to another male voice asking about him, states, "Ah, there he is! Returning from the hunt. A little hunter. Probably dragging prey." Upbeat, mischievous instrumental music begins as the beaver walks past the house. The dog, distressed, complains in a male voice, "I have nothing to do. Just pulling different dogs out of the river. And I didn't ask to be pulled out. I, I might not have been drowning at all. Or maybe I was just scuba diving!" A "thud" is heard, and the beaver exclaims, "Here you go! For our Sharik!" The dog looks relieved as the beaver stops. The boy returns, and the beaver and dog stand before him, the dog now happy. The music fades as the scene transitions to the interior of the house. The boy, cat, and dog are seated at a wooden table with a red-and-white checkered tablecloth, centered by a large golden samovar with a white teapot. The boy sips from a blue teacup while the cat, sitting on a wooden stool, pours a drink from a brown jug into a white mug with a gold rim and drinks from it. The dog, seated on a wooden chair with a yellow backrest, watches with a smile. The room contains a patchwork curtain, a wooden fireplace, and a framed still-life painting above the couch, creating a warm atmosphere as the characters enjoy tea time. The beaver's voice is heard sighing, "Oh, this Sharik..."

A large golden samovar with a white teapot sits at the center, flanked by blue teacups and a plate of bread. Whimsical, slightly melancholic music plays in the background. The gray striped cat, wearing a white scarf, animatedly gestures and speaks in a higher-pitched voice, pondering the dog's lack of hunting success, stating, "Ah, a hunter found. No income from you, only expenses," and then questioning, "What to do with him? He'll perish without hunting, shrivel up completely." As the cat pours a drink from the samovar into a white mug, it continues, suggesting, "Or maybe we should make him a sled dog, huh? We'll buy him a cart, we'll carry milk to the market on him. We'll cultivate the garden on him." The brown dog, named "Sharik" by the cat, sits on a wooden chair with a yellow backrest, watching with a mix of curiosity and concern, occasionally resting his chin on his paw. He responds in a deeper, melancholic voice, expressing resignation, "Ah, do whatever you want. Even a scarecrow. Life is not dear to me anyway." The red-haired boy, in his yellow sweater and blue pants, sips from his cup, listening intently and reacting with subtle expressions to the conversation. The cat takes a bite of bread, and the dog looks on thoughtfully. The cat then declares, "No, no, no, I've decided. I'll write a letter to mom and dad. Let them send a photo-gun for Sharik. That's hunting, and no need to kill animals." The background features a patchwork curtain, a wooden wall, and a yellow guitar. The music shifts to a more upbeat and adventurous tone as the scene abruptly transitions to a bright beach setting. Two striped deck chairs sit on golden sand under a radiant sun, with a sailboat visible on the blue ocean. The chairs are occupied by two figures, one holding a drink, both relaxing under palm fronds, suggesting a peaceful, sunny day by the sea.

event_blocks.summary: On a bright beach, a red-haired woman and a bearded man converse in Russian from striped deck chairs, the man suggesting a move to "Prostokvashino" and expressing concern for a boy, while the woman reassures him about the boy and his gray striped cat. The scene shifts to a dimly lit wooden room at night, where the gray striped cat sleeps on a blue chest and a red-haired boy sleeps under a patchwork quilt, with a black bird on a wardrobe; a melancholic brass tune transitions to an upbeat one as curtains flutter and a lantern appears outside. A wooden door creaks open, and a brown dog, identified as "Postman Sharik," enters holding the lantern, invited in by the now-awake red-haired boy. In a barn at night, Sharik illuminates the boy, the cat, a white cow with brown spots, and a small brown calf, excitedly announcing the calf's birth in Russian, prompting a cow's moo and male satisfaction, while the cat gestures and the boy looks surprised. Later, in the barn, Sharik, the boy (Uncle Fyodor), and Matroskin the cat debate ownership of the calf and cow; Sharik suggests taking the calf home, Matroskin agrees to take the cow too but argues they are state-owned due to renting the cow, while Uncle Fyodor asserts that their produce belongs to them. Matroskin uses a refrigerator analogy and insists on returning only one cow based on receipts, but an off-screen female voice reminds him of his intent to buy the cow and suggests taking both, as the segment ends amidst the barn's rustic atmosphere.

raw_segments: The video opens on a serene beach with a bright yellow sun, where a red-haired woman in a blue swimsuit and a shirtless man with a beard lounge in striped deck chairs, gesturing as they converse in Russian. The man suggests leaving their resort for "Prostokvashino," expressing concern for a boy, but the woman, speaking practically, dismisses his worries, highlighting the boy's well-being and his protective gray striped cat, leading the man to wistfully remark about having such a cat himself. The scene then cuts to a dimly lit wooden room at night, where a crescent moon is visible through a window. The gray striped cat sleeps atop a blue chest, and a red-haired boy sleeps under a patchwork quilt, while a black bird perches on a wardrobe. A melancholic brass instrument plays, transitioning to an upbeat brass band tune as curtains flutter and a lantern's light appears outside. A wooden door creaks open, and a brown dog enters, holding the lantern, identified by a boisterous male voice as "Postman Sharik, I mean, just Sharik!" The red-haired boy, now awake, invites him in with a child's voice. The scene shifts to a barn at night, where Sharik, still holding the lantern, illuminates the red-haired boy, the gray striped cat, a white cow with brown spots, and a small brown calf. Sharik excitedly announces in Russian that "Our cow has given birth to a calf!", prompting a cow's moo and a deeper male voice to express satisfaction about the cow, as the gray striped cat gestures excitedly and the red-haired boy looks on with a hand to his face, appearing surprised or concerned.

In a dimly lit barn at night, illuminated by a lantern, the brown dog, named Sharik, and the red-haired boy, addressed as Uncle Fyodor, stand together, engaged in a lively debate with the animated gray striped cat, Matroskin. Sharik, initially calm, gestures with his paw while speaking in a male voice, suggesting they take 'him' (referring to the calf) home. Matroskin, expressive and gesturing excitedly with both paws, responds in a distinct male voice, agreeing to take 'mom' (the cow) home too, but then emphatically argues that the white cow with brown spots is 'state-owned' because they rented her, and therefore the small brown calf, who stands beside her looking attentive and slightly nervous, is also 'state-owned.' Uncle Fyodor, listening attentively with a hand to his face, interjects in a slightly deeper male voice, telling Sharik to be quiet, and counters that while the cow is state-owned, everything she produces, like milk or calves, is theirs. Matroskin then uses an analogy of a rented refrigerator and its 'frost' to press his point, while Sharik argues they only rented one cow but now have two. Matroskin, pointing toward the cow and calf, insists they must adhere to receipts and reporting, stating they will only return one cow. An off-screen female voice then questions their argument, reminding Matroskin that he intended to buy the cow permanently and suggesting he should take both the cow and calf. Matroskin begins to respond as the segment ends, all within the rustic, enclosed atmosphere of the barn with its wooden walls and hay-covered floor, the lantern casting soft shadows on the group as they engage in this humorous dispute over ownership.

event_blocks.summary: In a dimly lit barn at night, a gray striped cat animatedly declared in Russian to a white cow with brown spots and a small brown calf that he would be "twice as happy" because he had "two cows," as lively accordion music began. The scene shifted to a sunny rural yard where a brown dog playfully chased a tumbling small brown calf. A man with a mustache and brown hat, wearing a yellow coat, called to the dog, "Gavryusha! To me!" as the calf and dog ran toward a rustic wooden house. The man, surprised, sat on the steps with a yellow book, commanding the dog, "Gavryusha! Lie down!" and "Voice!" prompting a bark, while a red-haired boy observed. After the man gestured and commanded, "Gavryusha! Get it!," a sound of impact was heard, and the man, holding a black hat, complained about it having "so many holes" after they were "butting." The boy walked away, and later, standing by an open window, asked the man, "And on what business did you come?" The man, identified as Postman Pechkin, later arrived at the house with a yellow-framed object, refusing to give a parcel to the boy and cat, stating they lacked documents, despite the cat's claim of "Whiskers and a tail!" and the dog's "Well, here are my documents!" A "boing" sound effect occurred, followed by a night sky scene with melancholic music. The boy, dog, and cat contemplated on the porch steps, and Pechkin reflected, "This is probably my photo-gun that arrived." The cat then proposed finding a similar box to Pechkin's to swap, and the boy exclaimed, "Hooray!" The dog barked when called, and a white cow appeared. Pechkin arrived again, and the cat, greeting him, stated they wouldn't take the parcel, asking, "Why would we need shoe polish?" Pechkin, confused, was told it was "ordinary, the kind you clean shoes with." After a camera click sound, Pechkin left, followed by the cat. Later, Pechkin questioned who sent so much boot polish, and the boy explained his uncle at a boot polish factory sent it. Pechkin refused to hand over the package, calling himself "not a fool," and the cat intervened in a calm female voice, suggesting they drink tea as "everything is on the table." The scene transitioned to a cozy wooden room with a patchwork curtain and a golden samovar on a wooden table covered with a red-and-white checkered tablecloth. The boy, cat, and dog were seated, and the boy invited Pechkin to sit. The cat poured tea, and they enjoyed a peaceful tea time with upbeat, traditional Russian folk music.

raw_segments: The minute opens inside a dimly lit barn at night, where a gray striped cat animatedly gestures with both paws toward a white cow with brown spots and a small brown calf, both looking on with wide eyes. The cat, speaking in an excited male voice, exclaims in Russian that he will never part with them, stating he was already happy but now will be "twice as happy" because he has "two cows." Upbeat, lively accordion music begins as the scene transitions to a bright, sunny rural yard with green grass, trees, and a wooden fence. A brown dog walks along a path, and suddenly the small brown calf tumbles playfully from behind a tree, rolling on the grass. The dog watches with amusement, then playfully nudges and chases the calf. A man with a mustache and brown hat, wearing a yellow coat, stands by a wooden fence, waving his hand and calling out "Gavryusha! To me!" to the dog. The calf runs toward a rustic wooden house with blue shutters and a thatched roof, followed by the dog. The man then sits on the house steps, holding a yellow book, looking surprised as the dog and calf approach. He calls out "Gavryusha! Lie down!" and later "Voice!", prompting the dog to bark. The red-haired boy, in a yellow sweater and blue pants, observes with a hand on his hip. The man gestures with his hands, seemingly scolding, and then commands "Gavryusha! Get it!". The accordion music intensifies, followed by a sound of something hitting. The man, now holding a black hat, speaks in a frustrated tone, complaining that he came "on business" but they were "butting," and his hat is now "not a hat, but just ventilation," with "so many holes." The boy then turns and walks away, and the man continues to gesture. The final shot shows the boy standing by the open window of the house, looking out thoughtfully, as the dog and calf are no longer in view, and the boy asks the man in a young voice, "And on what business did you come?".

The scene opens outside a rustic wooden house with blue shutters and a red roof, where a man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, stands holding a yellow-framed object. He gestures animatedly while speaking to a red-haired boy in a yellow sweater and blue pants, who listens with a hand on his hip. The man, in an energetic voice, declares he has brought a parcel but won't give it to them because they lack documents, adding that "tailed ones aren't supposed to have documents at all." The boy indignantly asks why he brought it then, to which the man explains it's "how it's supposed to be" but without documents, it can't be given away, threatening to "walk around like this for a whole week." The boy demands the parcel, prompting the man to ask about their documents. Beside them, a brown dog with floppy ears and a gray striped cat with white markings stand close, the dog looking concerned and the cat gesturing excitedly. The cat, in a high-pitched voice, exclaims "Whiskers and a tail!" as its documents, followed by the dog, in a deeper, slightly gruff voice, presenting "Well, here are my documents!" The man counters, asking if they have a "stamp on their tail," to which the cat sadly replies "No," and the man dismisses whiskers as faked. A "boing" sound effect punctuates the exchange. The man raises the yellow frame to his face as if taking a picture, then the scene cuts to a night sky with a crescent moon and stars above the house’s roof, accompanied by gentle, melancholic music. A sigh and a "whoosh" sound are heard. The next shot shows the boy, dog, and cat standing together on the porch steps, looking up thoughtfully, followed by another sigh. The dog then sits on the step, crossing his arms, while the cat sits beside him, both appearing contemplative. The man's voice returns, calmer, reflecting, "This is probably my photo-gun that arrived." The dog shifts his posture, looking down with a worried expression, and the cat raises a paw as if making a point, suggesting a moment of reflection or decision after the man’s departure, as the melancholic music continues to play.

The scene opens outside a rustic wooden house with blue shutters, where a brown dog and a gray striped cat stand together. The cat, gesturing excitedly, speaks in a high-pitched, animated voice, proposing, "Я вот что придумал! Мы такой же ящик найдем, как у Печкина! И когда он снова придет... Да ящики ему поменяем!" (I've come up with something! We'll find the same kind of box as Pechkin's! And when he comes again... We'll swap his boxes!). The red-haired boy appears, standing by the house with a hand on his hip, listening intently and responding with a questioning "Что?" (What?) before exclaiming "Ура!" (Hooray!) at the cat's plan. The dog looks concerned, then the boy calls out, "Гаврюша! Ко мне! Дай лапу!" (Gavryusha! Come here! Give me your paw!), to which the dog barks. The boy gestures toward the window, where a dark shape is visible inside, while the cat raises a paw as if making a point, and the dog sits on the step with arms crossed. The boy stands on the porch steps with the dog and cat, looking thoughtful. Suddenly, a white cow with brown spots appears, looking at the group with curiosity. Upbeat, playful music begins as the camera zooms out to show the entire house, with the cat peeking from the attic window, then climbing out and waving. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, arrives holding a yellow-framed object resembling a camera or projector. He approaches the door, and the cat peeks from the window, then opens the door and greets him with a wave. The man, identifying himself as "почтальон Печкин" (Postman Pechkin), states, "принес посылку. Только я вам ее не отдам, потому что у вас документов нету." (I brought a parcel. But I won't give it to you, because you don't have documents.). The cat, stepping out and waving cheerfully, retorts, "А мы бы эту посылку и сами у вас не взяли. Зачем это нам гуталин?" (And we wouldn't have taken this parcel from you ourselves. Why would we need shoe polish?), to which the confused postman asks, "Какой такой гуталин?" (What kind of shoe polish?), and the cat explains, "Обыкновенный, которым ботинки чистят." (Ordinary, the kind you clean shoes with.). The man raises the yellow-framed object to his face as if taking a picture, accompanied by a camera click sound. He then turns and walks away, and the cat follows him, waving goodbye. The final shot shows the cat standing in the doorway, waving with a happy expression, as the playful music continues.

The scene opens outside a rustic wooden house with blue shutters and a red roof, where a man with a mustache, brown hat, and long yellow coat, identified as 'Comrade Pechkin', gestures animatedly while speaking in a deep, gruff Russian accent, questioning who sent so much boot polish and if someone is opening a boot polish stand. He holds a yellow-framed object resembling a camera, which he raises to his face as if taking a picture. A gray striped cat with white stripes, white muzzle, and chest, emerges from the doorway, waving cheerfully and gesturing excitedly, joining a red-haired boy in a yellow sweater and blue pants on the porch. The boy, speaking in a younger, higher-pitched Russian accent, explains that his uncle, who lives at a boot polish factory, sent it, having an abundance of the product. Comrade Pechkin retorts that he won't hand over the package, calling himself 'not a fool,' prompting an assertive 'Who's there?' from the cat. Pechkin then calls someone a 'chatterbox,' to which the boy pleads not to offend 'our little bird.' The cat, now speaking in a calm, soothing female voice with a Russian accent, interjects, telling them not to be angry and to go drink tea, as 'everything is on the table.' The man gestures toward the boy and the cat before walking into the house, accompanied by the sound of a door closing, as gentle, melodic instrumental music begins. Inside, the setting shifts to a cozy wooden room with a patchwork curtain, a wooden fireplace, and a yellow guitar hanging on the wall. The boy, cat, and a brown dog with floppy ears and a white muzzle are seated at a wooden table covered with a red-and-white checkered tablecloth. A large golden samovar with a white teapot on top serves as the centerpiece. The boy, addressing the man as 'Comrade Pechkin,' invites him to sit, followed by the sound of chairs moving. The cat pours tea from the samovar into a white mug, while Comrade Pechkin sits at the table, holding a piece of food and a white teacup, with the cat watching him. The atmosphere is warm and inviting, with natural light streaming through the windows, and the characters appear to be enjoying a peaceful tea time together as the music transitions to an upbeat, traditional Russian folk tune.

event_blocks.summary: Inside a rustic wooden room, upbeat folksy instrumental music plays as a gray striped cat, Vasya, sits beside a man with a mustache, brown hat, and long yellow coat. The man animatedly gestures, suggesting, "Give her not those candies. They are very wonderful." A golden samovar with a white teapot rests on a red-and-white checkered tablecloth, and Vasya reaches for a blue teacup. The man raises a yellow-framed object, like a camera, to his face, then walks out the wooden door, followed by Vasya, who exclaims, "Give it back, give it back! This is my candy!" The man re-enters carrying a large yellow wooden box labeled in Cyrillic for 'Uncle Fyodor' and 'Sharik' from 'Dad and Mom' at a 'Black Sea Resort'. Identified as Uncle Fyodor, he places the box on the table, remarking, "Vasya, to what lengths people go, greedy for someone else's good!" Vasya excitedly jumps to inspect the box. A red-haired woman in a yellow sweater and black turtleneck enters, smiling as she reads the label. A brown dog, Sharik, sits on a wooden chair, holding a black camera and pointing it at the box. The woman pulls out a piece of paper and reads aloud, "Well, Sharik, now you will be engaged in photo hunting. You will photograph animals and send photos to various magazines." Sharik pragmatically responds, "Right, where they pay more." The woman continues reading, her expression delighting, "Don't be sad, Uncle Fyodor. Hold on, we'll be there soon. Only one dress left. Dad and Mom." The scene cuts to a blue rabbit running through a grassy field, chased by Sharik the dog with his camera, a playful pursuit accompanied by lively music. The chase transitions to a rural yard with wooden fences and a barn, where the woman is sawing a log, assisted by the cat. The dog bursts in, causing a large explosion from the log, startling the woman and the cat. The woman, holding the blue rabbit, looks annoyed, while the dog approaches sheepishly. A male voice explains in Russian he spent half the day chasing the rabbit to photograph it. The woman sarcastically suggests he'll now have to chase the dog for the photo. The red-haired man arrives with a green plaid suitcase, and the music shifts to a joyful fanfare as the woman joyfully embraces him, lifting the cat. A chorus shouts "Hooray!" in Russian. The dog looks on with longing before the man pets it, and the group stands together in a warm reunion against a rural landscape.

event_blocks.summary: In a sunny rural yard bordered by a wooden fence, with green hills and wooden houses in the background, upbeat accordion music plays as a man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, adjusts his hat and greets a red-haired woman in a blue suit with "Здравствуйте! С прибытием!" (Hello! Welcome!). She responds, "Здравствуйте, здравствуйте, дорогой Игорь Иванович!" (Hello, hello, dear Igor Ivanovich!), and after a playful exchange about kissing, the man complains about his hat, "Посмотрите, что они с моей шапкой сделали! Через неё теперь хорошо вермишель отбрасывать" (Look what they did to my hat! Now it's good for draining vermicelli through it). The woman, calling him "товарищ Печкин" (Comrade Pechkin), offers him new headwear. He then removes his hat, revealing a black ponytail, and dons a white sailor-style cap with 'ПОЧТА' (POST) on it, remarking on the transition from field to naval post. With a brown dog now in the woman's arms and a gray striped cat nearby, they watch the man salute. The man then approaches a rustic wooden house, knocks on the window, and offers to take a photo with his "photorifle," saying, "А давайте, я вас на память из фоторужья щёлкну!" (And let me snap you for memory with a photo-gun!). A woman with dark hair and a white hat looks out, and the man gestures. The brown dog, now holding a black camera, runs up and salutes the man, who returns it, humorously declining to be photographed himself with "Не надо меня из ружья щёлкать! Я может только жить начинаю. На пенсию перехожу" (Don't snap me with a gun! I might just be starting to live. I'm retiring). The man smiles as the woman inside waves. The scene concludes in a birch forest with a yellow bird chirping on a branch, as the camera pans to the sky, and the music fades.

A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, stands near the fence, adjusting his hat and gesturing animatedly, exclaiming "Здравствуйте! С прибытием!" (Hello! Welcome!). A red-haired woman in a blue suit approaches him, greeting him warmly, "Здравствуйте, здравствуйте, дорогой Игорь Иванович!" (Hello, hello, dear Igor Ivanovich!), and they exchange a brief, friendly interaction, with the man playfully asking, "Не хотите, гражданка, целоваться?" (Don't you want to kiss, citizen?) and then stating, "Давайте сначала конфликт разберём" (Let's first sort out the conflict). When she asks "Какой такой конфликт?" (What kind of conflict?), he complains, "Посмотрите, что они с моей шапкой сделали!" (Look what they did to my hat!), suggesting "Через неё теперь хорошо вермишель отбрасывать" (Now it's good for draining vermicelli through it). The woman, addressing him as "товарищ Печкин" (Comrade Pechkin), tries to calm him, eventually offering him a new "головной убор" (headwear). The man then removes his hat, revealing a long black ponytail, and places a white sailor-style cap with the Cyrillic text 'ПОЧТА' (POST) on his head, remarking, "Бывает ты почта полевая, а у нас морская будет" (Sometimes you're field post, but ours will be naval). The woman, now holding a brown dog, is joined by a gray striped cat, and they all look on as the man salutes. The scene shifts to the man approaching a rustic wooden house with blue shutters, where he knocks on the window, offering, "А давайте, я вас на память из фоторужья щёлкну!" (And let me snap you for memory with a photo-gun!). Inside, a woman with dark hair and a white hat peers out, and the man gestures toward her. The dog, now holding a black camera, runs up and salutes the man, who returns the salute, while the man humorously declines being photographed himself, stating, "Не надо меня из ружья щёлкать! Я может только жить начинаю. На пенсию перехожу" (Don't snap me with a gun! I might just be starting to live. I'm retiring). The man then looks through the window again, smiling, as the woman inside waves back. The scene cuts to a birch forest with a yellow bird perched on a branch, chirping, as the camera pans upward to the sky, ending the sequence on a peaceful natural note, with the whimsical music fading out.

event_blocks.summary: An upbeat, folksy instrumental melody accompanies a sunlit forest scene with birch trees and yellow birds, transitioning to a rustic wooden house where a man with a mustache and brown hat greets a red-haired woman holding a brown dog, with a white cow nearby.
