raw_segments: The video opens with an upbeat, folksy instrumental melody, featuring a balalaika-like sound, accompanying a vibrant, sunlit forest scene with birch trees and yellow birds, establishing a cheerful, pastoral atmosphere. The camera pans right to reveal a rustic wooden house with blue shutters and a wooden fence, where a man with a mustache and brown hat, carrying a white box with black squares, greets a red-haired woman in a blue suit who holds a brown dog. A white cow with brown spots stands calmly nearby. The music continues as the scene shifts to a field where a red-haired boy rides a small brown calf, while a man in a white shirt digs with a shovel, accompanied by a gray striped cat. The camera then focuses on the red-haired woman standing in a lush flower garden, holding a bouquet of white and blue flowers, with butterflies fluttering around her. She smiles and gestures warmly, and her voice is heard saying in Russian, "Yes. I understood a lot. And if I had a second life, I would spend it here. In Prostokvashino." The scene transitions to a bright blue sky with white clouds, where colorful Cyrillic text appears: 'ВСЕ САМЫЕ ЛЮБИМЫЕ МУЛЬТИКИ ИЗ ДЕТСТВА' (All the most beloved cartoons from childhood), followed by 'И многое, многое другое! Подписывайся!' (And much, much more! Subscribe!). An excited child's voice enthusiastically proclaims, "All the most beloved cartoons from childhood on the Soyuzmultfilm channel! Subscribe!" as the screen turns solid blue, displaying the 'СОЮЗМУЛЬТФИЛЬМ' logo in white, followed by the title 'ЗИМА В ПРОСТОКВАШИНО' (Winter in Prostokvashino) in bold white letters. The upbeat music fades, replaced by a distinct, melancholic instrumental piece, possibly featuring a guitar and harmonica, as the final shot reveals a serene, snow-covered village with wooden houses, snow-laden trees, and gentle snowfall, setting a peaceful winter mood.

Inside a rustic wooden room with log walls, a striped rug, and a red-and-white checkered tablecloth, a lively, whimsical folk tune plays. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, stands holding a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА' (POST). A small black bird with a yellow beak flutters erratically on the floor, startling the man who exclaims "Ну и ну!" (Well, well!) and "Что делается?" (What's going on?). A gray striped cat, wearing a red hat and scarf, runs around excitedly, then, after a brief moment of confusion, grabs the bird and tosses it aside, turning to the man with a mischievous expression. The man, looking surprised and slightly alarmed, gestures with his hand as he laments, "На дворе конец двадцатого века, а у нас в доме одна пара валенок на двоих. Ну, как при царе Горохе!" (It's the end of the 20th century outside, and in our house, there's one pair of felt boots for two. Like in the time of Tsar Gorokh!). He continues, asking, "А почему так вышло-то? Что у вас средств нету? То есть, денег у вас не хватает?" (But why did it turn out this way? Do you not have funds? That is, you don't have enough money?), to which a slightly different voice responds, "Средства у нас есть." (We have funds.). The man then declares, "У нас ума не хватает." (We lack intelligence/sense.), placing his postal bag on the floor and stepping back exasperatedly. The cat pulls at its red scarf, seemingly trying to adjust it, while the bird scurries away. The man raises his hand again, continuing his monologue, "Говорил я Тимохотнику: 'Купи себе валенки!' А он что?" (I told Timokhotnik: 'Buy yourself felt boots!' And what did he do?), with the other voice interjecting, "Что он?" (What did he do?). The man concludes, "Пошёл и кеды купил! Они говорят: 'Красивые!' И он, не подумавши, и сделал. У нас зимой национальная деревенская одежда какая? Валенки, штаны ватные, тулуп и шапка." (He went and bought sneakers! They say: 'They're beautiful!' And he did it without thinking. What is our national village clothing in winter? Felt boots, padded pants, a sheepskin coat, and a hat.). The scene, featuring a window with pink floral curtains and a view of green trees, a patchwork curtain, and a wooden door, maintains a chaotic yet humorous atmosphere with exaggerated expressions and movements.

The scene opens inside a cozy wooden room with rustic log walls, accompanied by whimsical, comedic background music. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, stands near the door, holding a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА' (POST). He gestures animatedly, speaking in a deep, expressive Russian voice, initially scolding someone as a 'blockhead' and urging them to 'open their eyes' to understand. A small black bird with a yellow beak flies in and perches on the window sill, then flutters near the man, but without making any distinct sounds. The man then turns to a gray striped cat, named Шарик, who is seated at a wooden table, wearing a red hat and scarf. The cat, with a higher-pitched, slightly whiny Russian voice, complains that they haven't spoken for 'two days,' which the man corrects to 'three.' The cat then suggests sending a telegram or letter, to which the man, holding his 'ПОЧТА' bag, explains the purpose of mail. He asks the cat if he wants a 'simple or congratulatory' form, and the cat, adjusting its scarf, insists on a 'simple' one, complaining about being spoiled and not needing anything from the man. As the cat begins writing with a yellow pencil on a large sheet of paper, the man explains he only has 'congratulatory' forms. The cat, looking annoyed, sighs about 'extending expenses,' but the man, now holding a long black pole, eventually concedes. The cat listens, then looks up with a mischievous grin, holding the pencil. The man then raises his hand, holding the black bag again, and in a cooing tone, calls the cat 'Шарик, you're a good boy,' only for the cat to immediately correct him, stating 'Incorrect.' The lively and humorous atmosphere is underscored by the exaggerated expressions and movements typical of classic animation, accompanied by the continuous whimsical music.

event_blocks.summary: In a dimly lit wooden room with a patchwork curtain, a gray striped cat wearing a red hat and scarf sat at a wooden table, writing with a yellow pencil. A man with a mustache, brown hat, and yellow coat entered with a black 'ПОЧТА' bag, stating that a congratulatory addressee must first be congratulated. The cat sarcastically responded, 'I congratulate you, little ball!' followed by a 'boing' and 'thwack.' A brown dog in a blue shirt and cowboy hat peeked through a window, exclaiming, 'You idiot!' The cat meowed, and the man asked what to write next. The cat suggested the weather, which the dog declared good, but the cat countered that a two-day blizzard had ruined hunting. The man told 'Comrade Dog' to be quiet and write about his own weather, to which the dog threatened to throw a poker. The man explained they would pack the poker, as it was sent as a parcel, and whimsical accordion music began. Later, the man, holding the 'ПОЧТА' bag, exclaimed in exasperation, 'What-what-what?' and threatened to launch an iron. The cat continued writing, and the man noted, 'More than a kilogram,' then declared, 'This is a parcel... Now we will deliver it.' He mused about container shipping if a barrel was rolled onto it. The scene shifted to a winter exterior with a snow-covered wooden house, snow-laden trees, and snowfall, revealing a wider snow-covered village. This transitioned to a framed painting of the house in summer, hanging in a living room with vibrant flowers, a blue toy car that made 'clunk' and 'squeak' sounds, a green couch, and a small TV. A red-haired man and boy interacted with the toy car, while a red-haired woman entered, holding a potted plant, wearing a blue suit and sunglasses, and said, 'How tired I am of all this!' A calendar read 'January 30.' Later, the red-haired woman, distressed with a wilting plant, entered the living room, remarking, 'Our apartment reminds me of a TV show. What? Where? When? It's called.' The calendar flipped from 'December 30' to 'December 31.' She added, 'And you won't understand what's lying where and when all this will end,' placing the plant down with a clatter. In the living room with red-striped walls and a green couch, the red-haired man and boy played with the blue toy car, the man pretending to fix its hood. The man then held a black tire to his face like a mask. The woman, now in a yellow armchair, accused, 'Because of your gasoline, all my flowers have wilted.' The man calmly replied, 'Flowers usually wilt in homes where the atmosphere is very strict.' The woman retorted, 'But ours is too cheerful,' listing their activities like ping-pong, chess, and buying an old Zaporozhets. The boy offered her a blue cup, which she drank from. The man conspiratorially stated, 'Uncle Fyodor and I decided... that it is absolutely necessary for us to get a second child somewhere. To remove strictness and anger.' The woman responded with a startled 'Oh!' On New Year's Eve, the red-haired woman in a yellow armchair, holding a blue cup, stated, 'Never!' and expressed a desire to go to Prostokvashino, but couldn't due to a performance. The red-haired boy suggested she take the last electric train. The woman humorously declined, not wanting to travel in a concert dress. The red-haired man, with a long beard, entered, agreeing it was true and suggesting she wear a 'concert quilted jacket... and concert felt boots' due to the cold in Prostokvashino. The woman wondered about Sharik and Matroskin. The man then placed a wooden box labeled 'Ministry of Communications, Prostokvashino Branch' on a table near a bookshelf, opening it with a distinct sound.

A gray striped cat, wearing a red hat with a white pom-pom and a red scarf, sits at a wooden table, focused on writing with a yellow pencil on a large sheet of paper. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, enters through a wooden door, holding a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА' (POST), and his deep, theatrical voice states that if the form is congratulatory, the addressee must first be congratulated. The cat, with a higher-pitched, whiny, and sarcastic voice, responds with a theatrical 'I congratulate you, little ball!' followed by a 'boing' and 'thwack' sound. Suddenly, the camera cuts to a window where a brown dog, dressed in a blue shirt and a cowboy hat, peeks in with a surprised look, reacting with a gruff 'You idiot!' The cat then lets out a 'meow' before the man, gesturing animatedly, asks what to write next. The cat suggests writing about the weather, to which the dog, with a deep, gruff voice, declares the weather is good. The cat immediately retorts that it's not good, as a blizzard has been howling for two days, ruining all hunting. The man then scolds the dog, telling 'Comrade Dog' to be quiet and write about his own weather when he replies. The video opens inside a rustic wooden room where a man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, stands near a door, holding a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА' (POST). He appears surprised and exasperated, exclaiming "Что-что-что?" (What-what-what?) and playfully threatening, "Да я занесу в него утюгом запустить!" (I'll launch an iron at him!). A gray striped cat, wearing a red hat with a white pom-pom and a red scarf, sits at a wooden table writing with a yellow pencil on a large sheet of paper, accompanied by a distinct writing sound. The man then calms, saying "Минуточку." (Just a minute.) and noting, "Больше килограмма." (More than a kilogram.), followed by a "thump" sound as he holds up a black object resembling a camera or projector, labeled 'ПОЧТА', and gestures excitedly, declaring, "Это посылка, получается. Этим транс-агентство занимается, сельской местности." (And if you roll a barrel onto it? That's already container shipping, it turns out. A transport agency deals with this, in rural areas.). The scene opens with upbeat, whimsical background music as a red-haired woman, looking distressed and holding a wilting potted plant, enters the living room from the kitchen. She gestures in frustration, remarking in a slightly sarcastic tone, "Наша квартира мне телевизионную передачу напоминает. Что? Где? Когда? Называется." (Our apartment reminds me of a TV show. What? Where? When? It's called.) The calendar on the wall squeaks as it flips from '30 ДЕКАБРЯ' to '31 ДЕКАБРЯ'. Her expression shifts to exasperation as she adds, "А не поймёшь, что где валяется и когда всё это кончится," (And you won't understand what's lying where and when all this will end,) before placing the plant down with a clatter. The view cuts to a vibrant living room with red-striped walls and a green couch, where a red-haired man and a red-haired boy are playfully interacting with a blue toy car, which makes an engine sound as the man opens its hood, pretending to fix it while the boy watches amused. The man then picks up a black tire, holding it up to his face like a mask with a whimsical sound effect, playfully gesturing toward the boy, who laughs. The woman re-enters, now seated on a yellow armchair, and in a frustrated, accusatory tone, states, "От вашего бензина у меня все цветы завяли." (Because of your gasoline, all my flowers have wilted.) The man calmly replies, "Они не от бензина завяли. Цветы обычно в тех домах вянут, где атмосфера очень строгая." (They didn't wilt from gasoline. Flowers usually wilt in homes where the atmosphere is very strict.) The woman retorts sarcastically, "А у нас она слишком весёлая," (But ours is too cheerful,) listing their activities, "Вы с дядей Фёдором и в пинг-понг, и в шахматы играете, и Запорожец старый купили, развалины. Уж и не знаю, чего вам ещё не хватает." (You and Uncle Fyodor play ping-pong and chess, and you bought an old Zaporozhets, a ruin. I don't even know what else you're missing.) The boy approaches her with a blue cup, from which she drinks, her demeanor softening. The man then conspiratorially states, "Мы с дядей Фёдором посоветовались и решили... что нам совершенно необходимо где-то второго ребёнка достать. Чтобы строгость снять и сердитость." (Uncle Fyodor and I consulted and decided... that it is absolutely necessary for us to get a second child somewhere. To remove strictness and anger.) The woman responds with a startled "Ой!" (Oh!) as the boy stands content beside her, capturing a lighthearted, domestic moment amidst their cozy, colorful living room.

The scene opens in a cozy living room with red-striped walls, where the red-haired woman, wearing a blue vest over a white blouse and green sunglasses, sits in a yellow armchair, sipping from a blue cup. The calendar on the wall reads '31 ДЕКАБРЯ' (December 31), indicating New Year’s Eve. She emphatically states "Никогда!" (Never!) before expressing a desire to go to Prostokvashino for New Year's, but quickly adds, "А я не могу. У меня выступление на новогоднем голубом огоньке" (But I can't. I have a performance on the New Year's Blue Light). The red-haired boy enters from the kitchen, holding the same blue cup, and approaches her with a concerned expression, suggesting, "А ты садись в последнюю электричку и приезжай к нам" (Then you take the last electric train and come to us). The woman, gesturing animatedly, humorously replies that while she loves nature, it's "не до такой степени, чтобы в концертном платье в электричках разъезжать" (not to such an extent that I'd travel around in electric trains in a concert dress). Suddenly, the red-haired man enters from the right, wearing a green coat with black cuffs and a long beard, smiling warmly, and agrees, "Это верно" (That's true). He places a hand on the woman’s shoulder, and she turns to him, her expression softening. He adds that "В Простоквашино сейчас мороз" (It's freezing in Prostokvashino now) and humorously suggests wearing "концертную телогрейку... и концертные валенки" (a concert quilted jacket... and concert felt boots). The woman then expresses concern, "Ой, интересно, как там мои Шарик с Матроскиным" (Oh, I wonder how my Sharik and Matroskin are doing there). The man exits the frame, reappears holding a wooden box with Cyrillic text, and places it on a table near a bookshelf. As he opens the box, a distinct sound of handling and opening is heard, and he reads the label aloud: "Министерство связи, Простоквашинское отделение" (Ministry of Communications, Prostokvashino Branch). The woman and boy watch him with curiosity, suggesting it contains an important delivery or gift for the New Year’s Eve celebration.

event_blocks.summary: In a warmly lit wooden room, a red-haired man with a beard, dressed in a green coat, places a wooden box labeled 'Ministry of Communications, Prostokvashino Branch' onto a table with a green patterned cloth, beside a green rotary phone. A small black bird with a yellow beak chirps and squawks from atop the box, retrieves a white paper, which the man reads aloud as a distorted voice announces, "It's me, Postman Pechkin, I brought a furshnay, furfivka." The man continues reading, "Sharik and Matroskin are starting to divide property," with the bird interjecting sounds like "Tyacheka!" and "Zapytat!" as the man adds, "Soon they'll start sawing the stove... And then the hut," concluding with "Postman Pechkin" and another "Tyacheka!" followed by a springy sound and a clunk. The scene shifts to a snowy village outside a rustic wooden house, with sounds of a rotary phone dialing and a fax machine. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and yellow coat, enters a dimly lit wooden room with a patchwork curtain and a red-and-white checkered tablecloth, carrying a black bag labeled 'POST'. He addresses a gray striped cat, wearing a red hat and scarf, who is writing with a yellow pencil, stating, "Oh, how good that you came, I need to send an urgent telegram to Sharik. Please, fill out the form. Felt-tip pen. Uncle Fyodor will arrive soon. Full stop. Urgently take my valenki and..." The cat looks up mischievously. In the same room, the postal worker speaks of '14 words and delivery' and '50 kopecks,' holding up a yellow-framed object. He shows a paper to a brown dog in a white robe and cowboy hat, sitting on a stone fireplace. The dog, after a surprised 'Mmm?', jumps down and, to the sound of pencil scratching, draws a teepee and a Native American figure on the wall. The man then turns to the gray striped cat, who protests, "I won't! I have no money!" The man retorts, "Look in your pockets!" to which the cat insists, "But I have no pockets!" The dog proudly declares, "I'll draw him an answer!" The man, holding the yellow-framed object again, raises it as if taking a picture, while the cat questions, "So what is this? What kind of folk art is this?" The dog explains, "This is an Indian national folk hut! It's called a wigwam!" The cat sighs, lamenting, "We, one might say, found him in a dump, washed him, cleaned him thoroughly, and he draws us a wigwam!" adding, "Ugh, Uncle Fyodor would have called a turtle instead of you for the box!" The man, holding a white box with black squares, gestures toward the alarmed, frantically waving cat, while the dog looks on with pride and concern, the atmosphere tense and curious, accompanied by whimsical, melancholic accordion and string music.

A man with a mustache and brown hat, wearing a 'ПОЧТА' hat and carrying a matching bag, enters and challenges them to guess his identity, which the cat correctly identifies as 'Admiral Ivan Fyodorovich Kruzenshtern, man and steamboat!' The man then reveals himself as 'Postman Pechkin,' presenting a 'Murzilka' magazine, a yellow-framed object, a white box with black squares, and black fabric, eliciting cheers. A red-haired man in a blue coat, holding a white chicken mask and a black bird, interacts with the dog, explaining that Uncle Fyodor is in a snowed-in car. The Postman drives the blue car through the snow, while the red-haired man, dog, and cat are pulled on a sled, accompanied by a black bird and upbeat music. raw_segments: Inside a cozy, dimly lit wooden room with rustic walls and a checkered tablecloth, a mischievous gray striped cat, wearing a red hat and scarf, sits writing with a yellow pencil. A low, raspy male voice, belonging to the cat, muses that it's not the clock striking, but someone knocking on the door. A deeper male voice, from the brown dog perched on the fireplace, invites them in. Suddenly, a man with a mustache, wearing a yellow coat and a 'ПОЧТА' hat, enters, carrying a matching bag. He greets them with a booming, theatrical voice, challenging them to guess his identity. The cat's raspy voice confidently declares him 'Admiral Ivan Fyodorovich Kruzenshtern, man and steamboat!' The dog's deeper voice dismisses this, while the cat repeatedly asks 'Who's there?' The man with the mustache, now adopting a higher-pitched, almost childlike voice, reveals himself as 'Postman Pechkin' and announces he's brought the 'Murzilka' magazine, prompting cheers of 'Hooray!' from the cat and dog. He pulls out a yellow-framed object and a white box with black squares, then holds up a black fabric. A red-haired man in a blue coat, holding a white chicken mask and a black bird, gestures animatedly, then interacts with the dog. The dog's voice asks about Uncle Fyodor, and the red-haired man's voice explains that he's in the car and they can't get through because the road is snowed in. The scene transitions to a serene exterior night view of a snow-covered village under a starry sky, with gentle snowfall, as upbeat, folksy instrumental music begins. A signpost reads 'ПРОСТОКВАШИНО 500 м.' A blue car drives through the snow, with the Postman inside, smiling, while the red-haired man, the dog, and the cat are pulled behind on a sled, and the black bird flies alongside, all accompanied by the cheerful music.

The minute opens with a snowy night scene where a gray striped cat, wearing a red Santa hat and scarf, and a brown dog, dressed in a light blue robe, struggle to pull a wooden sled through falling snow, accompanied by the sound of the sled dragging. Over a cheerful, slightly whimsical instrumental tune, an old man's voice with a deep, slightly raspy Russian accent expresses surprise about "sled cats" after mentioning "sled dogs." The dog appears exhausted, while the cat looks determined. The scene shifts to a red-haired man, wearing a blue coat with a fur-lined hood and a red scarf, leaning against a blue car, shivering and looking distressed as heavy snowflakes fall. The old man's voice continues, asking if "sled postmen" have been encountered, to which a second man, with a calm, slightly gruff Russian accent, responds, describing the harsh winter roads and weather, claiming that even "sled academics" are now seen, and he's witnessed it himself. The camera then pans to a distant, snow-covered wooden house with warm lights glowing from its windows, nestled among snow-laden trees. The next shot shows the red-haired woman, smiling as she drives the same blue car, with a black bird with a yellow beak perched on the hood. A young boy's excited voice shouts "Hey, stop! Stop!" then exclaims that the car has gotten out of the pit and he can proceed on his own. Inside the cozy, warmly lit house, a roaring fire burns in the stone fireplace. The cheerful instrumental music transitions into a melancholic acoustic guitar melody, as the gray striped cat, still in his festive attire, joyfully strums a yellow guitar while seated on a wooden stool beside a table covered with a red-and-white checkered tablecloth, a golden samovar, and a bowl of white marshmallows. A male voice sings in Russian, expressing a feeling of being "tamed" and no longer dreaming of seas, as television has replaced nature. The red-haired boy, wearing a yellow sweater, holds a blue cup and stands beside a banner with large blue Cyrillic letters spelling 'НОВЫМ' (NEW), as the black bird flies above. Finally, the brown dog, now wearing a white chef’s hat, stands proudly in front of the fireplace, holding a long string of sausages, his expression cheerful and proud.

The scene opens in a cozy, dimly lit wooden room with rustic charm, featuring a patchwork curtain and a roaring stone fireplace. A man with a mustache, wearing a long yellow coat and a green scarf, sits contemplatively on a wooden chair, holding a black hat, and sings a melancholic Russian song about not being recognized. He then points animatedly off-screen. The focus shifts to a red-haired man with a beard, in a light green tunic, who stands near a dusty, spider-webbed small TV, holding a small black object and gesturing as he comments in a deeper, conversational Russian voice about a "strange test pattern" on the TV. A gray striped cat, named Matroskin, wearing a red Santa hat and scarf, joyfully playing a yellow guitar on a wooden stool beside a table with a red-and-white checkered tablecloth, a golden samovar, and a bowl of marshmallows nearby, responds in a higher-pitched, animated Russian voice, clarifying that the TV's appearance is due to cobwebs, not a test pattern, and that "they have such a pattern on every pot... because they didn't talk." The scene cuts to a brown dog in a white chef’s hat, standing proudly before the roaring fireplace with sausages, who then speaks in a deeper, cheerful Russian voice, announcing "But we've already made up" and explaining it happened "when Uncle Fyodor was pulling out snow," followed by a chuckle. The red-haired man reappears, holding a knife and pointing at the TV, as the cat's voice returns, stating "Because joint labor for my benefit, it unites." The man in the yellow coat reenters, holding a long black bag, looking surprised, and calls out "Matroskin!" as the cat makes a "mrrp" sound. Upbeat accordion music begins. The cat then stops playing, points, and gestures excitedly. The dog reappears, stirring a blue pot. The cat resumes playing, then stops again, pointing and smiling. A stormy night with heavy rain and wind is seen through a window, adding concern as the man in the yellow coat stands near a wooden door, looking worried. The red-haired man continues his repair work on the TV, while the man in the yellow coat sits, pointing and speaking animatedly. The red-haired man gestures by the TV, and the man in the yellow coat points back. The cat points and smiles, the dog stirs its pot, and the cat plays and points again. The red-haired man, over the accordion music, expresses happiness that "everything ended well" and wishes a "pleasant New Year," to which the man in the yellow coat asks, "And you, won't you be with us for it?" The scene ends with the man in the yellow coat looking worried by the door, and the red-haired man gesturing by the TV, all within the warm, rustic wooden room.

event_blocks.summary: A man in a yellow coat, green scarf, and black beret, holding a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА', stands by a wooden door, expressing concern and asking about table decorations, dismissing the red-haired boy's suggestion of flowers with 'Bushes!' He points to a TV set, lamenting it shows cobwebs, and threatens to leave, but the boy pleads for him to stay, promising to fix the TV for their mother's appearance on 'Blue Light'. In a wooden room, a red-haired man holding a black bird agrees to fix the TV, and the bird perches on his hand before flying to his head. The man in the yellow coat then suggests decorating a live Christmas tree, but the boy worries about lacking ornaments, proposing they use old items from the attic. Upbeat, jazzy instrumental music begins as the scene shifts to a snowy night with a decorated Christmas tree. A brown dog in a white robe and cowboy hat holds a rifle as the man's voice announces a 'photo hunt', and the dog aims the rifle amidst snowfall. Later, the man in the yellow coat, holding the 'ПОЧТА' bag, asks about Moscow news. The scene returns to the boy and a gray striped cat with a red Santa hat, joined by a man in a blue coat who playfully announces he and his dad are getting a second child. The man in the yellow coat excitedly points to a small black TV set showing a man in a tuxedo and woman in a white dress, believing it's their mother's performance, but the man in the blue coat clarifies it's the leader of her amateur group. The boy anticipates his mother singing, but the man in the yellow coat reveals the TV's sound is broken, disappointing the boy. The scene transitions to the man in the yellow coat outside with the TV, handing it to the man in the blue coat, who places it on the snow. The group, including the boy, cat, and a brown dog in a cowboy hat, watches the TV with joy as snow falls.

raw_segments: The scene opens with a man with a mustache, wearing a long yellow coat, green scarf, and black beret, standing near a wooden door, holding a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА'. He appears concerned as he states in a deep, slightly gruff voice, "Don't persuade me. Nowadays, the main decoration of the table, what?" A red-haired boy, in a yellow sweater, sitting at a table with a gray striped cat wearing a red Santa hat, suggests in a higher-pitched voice, "Flowers?" The man dismisses, "Bushes!" He then excitedly gestures and points, adding, "TV, and it shows you cobwebs!" and threatens, "I'll go to my place." The boy, looking surprised, pleads, "Don't leave! We will definitely fix the TV! After all, today they will show our mom on the 'Blue Light'!" The man, now in a cozy wooden room with a red-haired man holding a small black bird, agrees, "Of course, we'll fix it!" as the bird perches on the man's hand and then flies to his head. The man with the mustache continues, "And in the yard there is a beautiful Christmas tree, live and real! Let's decorate it!" The boy excitedly exclaims, "Hooray! Let's!" but then becomes distressed, "Only we don't have toys, we have nothing to decorate with!" The man questions, "How? How nothing?" The boy then suggests, "But we have many old things in the attic! Let's decorate the tree with them!" As upbeat, whimsical, slightly jazzy instrumental music begins, the scene transitions to a snowy night outside, showing a decorated Christmas tree adorned with various ornaments. The camera pans to a brown dog in a white robe and cowboy hat, holding a rifle in the snow. The man's voice returns, formally announcing, "Attention, please! Please make smart faces. Photo hunt on you!" as the dog raises its rifle, aiming forward amidst gentle snowfall.

The scene opens with a close-up of a gray striped cat, wearing a red Santa hat and scarf, standing beside a young boy in a brown winter coat and fur hat, both under a snow-covered Christmas tree adorned with ornaments, as snow falls gently. They look up with wonder and slight concern, accompanied by whimsical, accordion-like music. The camera cuts to a man in a yellow coat and black beret, peering out of a rustic wooden house window, holding a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА' (POST). He speaks in a deep voice, asking, "What's the news in Moscow?" The scene returns to the cat and boy, now joined by a tall man with a long nose and beard in a blue winter coat, who, in a higher voice, playfully responds, "Dad and I decided to get a second child." The man in the yellow coat, still speaking in a deep voice, exclaims excitedly, "Oh! What joy! On TV, an uncle with a big mustache gave your mom flowers!" He then declares, "I'll show you this uncle now!" as he holds up a small black TV set displaying a man in a tuxedo and a woman in a white dress. The man in the blue coat laughs, clarifying, "That's not a fellow at all. That's the leader of mom's amateur performance group." The boy, in a child's voice, excitedly exclaims, "Oh! Now our mom is going to sing!" The man in the yellow coat then reveals, "Only this is all in vain. Your TV's sound doesn't work!" The boy, disappointed, laments, "Oh, what a pity we can't hear mom!" The scene shifts to the man in the yellow coat outside with the TV, as the man in the blue coat, the boy, and the cat approach him. He hands the TV to the man in the blue coat, who smiles and places it on the snow-covered ground. The man in the yellow coat gestures toward the TV, which again shows the man in the tuxedo and woman in the white dress. The final shot shows the group—man in blue coat, boy, cat, and a brown dog wearing a cowboy hat—standing together in the snow, looking at the TV with joy and anticipation, as snow continues to fall around them, all set to the whimsical music.

event_blocks.summary: A red-haired woman in a blue ski suit, standing before a snow-covered Christmas tree with falling snowflakes, states in Russian, "I prepared for this performance for half a year." The scene shifts to a man in a yellow coat and black beret outside a wooden house, holding a small black TV set showing a woman in a white dress and a man in a tuxedo, exclaiming in Russian, "Wow! Your mom is being broadcast both there and here. How far technology has come." The red-haired woman clarifies, "It's not technology that got here, but I myself got here, on skis. And the concert is recorded on tape." Returning to her, she waves cheerfully as voices shout "Ura!" and "Hello, dear friends!" A man in a blue coat with a fur collar, a boy in a brown winter coat, a gray striped cat in a red Santa hat and scarf, and a brown dog, all wave happily outside the house. The red-haired woman's voice adds, "I told you I just can't live without our Prostokvashino." She then stands with the boy and the cat under the Christmas tree, smiling as snowflakes fall, accompanied by a cheerful, upbeat, folksy song with a male vocalist and chorus about winter joys. This music continues through blue title cards with white Cyrillic text listing production credits, including screenwriter E. Uspensky, director V. Popov, and composer E. Krylatov, concluding with the copyright notice 'СОЮЗМУЛЬТФИЛЬМ, 1980г.'
